votes.ruling: UPHOLD the model (C-hybrid, files+git canonical, single-live-writer + mkdir-lock, one-way derived layers, additive-merge demoted to manual recovery, MM2-canonical re-openable only behind the operator's own flip-trigger) — but CORRECT one over-claim: the durability headline "strictly more durable than (A): two recovery layers vs one opaque store" is not literally defensible as written and must be made CONDITIONAL.
votes.reason: The storage MODEL survives scrutiny and is corroborated, not refuted: (1) External best practice backs the shape exactly — Single-Source-of-Truth ("one authoritative store... eliminates duplicated records that creep into side tables/CSV exports/ad-hoc caches; treat each connected store as a publishing destination") and the anti-bidirectional finding ("LWW is notoriously prone to data loss"; "the last writer wins, silently destroying the other's work"). Jack's own manual documents this cost in plain text ("GDrive wins on conflict -> Metamate's changes LOST", additive-only -> unbounded bloat + forever-manual cleanup), and his bidirectional merge exists to serve CONCURRENT multi-daily-driver writes (Metamate+Claude Code+Codex) — a precondition the solo AM lacks, so the merge's reason-to-exist is absent for one writer. (2) The ruling is NOT a rubber-stamp: it OVERTURNS the operator's APPROVED newest decision (REV3 = MM2-canonical) back to files+git, with reasons — the opposite of deference. (3) It beats (A) REV3 on durability-of-canonical because REV3 bets the CANONICAL copy on a late-beta store with, BY THE OPERATOR'S OWN ADMISSION, an unverified ~100KB/file cap + truncation (one oversized narrative.md = silent loss), opaque version-recovery/retention, and a known ~23-mission silent-stop bug; and REV3 contradicts the operator's OWN ship-now recommendation (§11 "Now -> option 3", laptop files+git on-demand, which IS file-canonical). (4) It correctly decouples WHERE-STATE-LIVES from WHAT-WAKES-THE-LOOP (REV3 conflated them): ship option-3 on-demand now for immediate value; make the 07:00 unattended fire a hosted NUDGE later (Phase 3 ii) without canonical state living in the scheduler. (5) The mkdir lock is sound per external evidence (atomic at kernel level; ruling already uses trap-release + age-based staleness + excludes .locks/ from projection), with only the NFS-atomicity caveat, which is moot for a local-process FUSE mount. So no alternative model is genuinely better. HOWEVER, one genuine evidence-backed weakness lands and forces refuted=true under the rubric: the durability HEADLINE is stated unconditionally, and production cloud-mount evidence refutes it as an absolute — rclone forum: "cloud storage is far less reliable than file systems expect," rclone mount "can't use retries" the way sync can, and "connecting applications directly to rclone mounts can make those applications highly unstable"; plus a documented "Google Drive Sync corrupting git repositories" failure class; plus the operator's OWN untested VERIFY-1 (git-inside-the-mclone-mount "NOT YET TESTED"). Therefore files-canonical is only more durable than (A) WHEN git lives on LOCAL DISK (not on the cloud mount), and the claimed "two independent recovery layers" is only fully true once GDrive revision-history-under-git is verified to retain usable history for mounted .md — the operator flags exactly this concern for MM2 but has NOT applied the same skepticism to GDrive's revision layer. On an unattended FUSE host, git-on-mount could itself corrupt, so the durability advantage is conditional, not strict. The ruling already mitigates structurally (Phase-1 git on laptop local disk; "git on local disk + sync markdown to mount" stated as the contingency; defers the mount-host to Phase 3), so this is a WORDING/INVARIANT correction, not a model change.
votes.better_alternative: Keep model (C) exactly as specified, with three precise corrections so the ruling is literally defensible and implementation-safe: (1) Soften the headline from "strictly more durable than (A)" to "more durable than (A) for the canonical copy PROVIDED git's object store lives on LOCAL DISK, never on the cloud FUSE mount" — and ADD it as a hard invariant L1-DUR: '.git/ MUST reside on local disk; the GDrive/mclone mount carries the working tree (.md) only; if the canonical host is ever a FUSE mount, git stays on local disk and markdown is one-way synced TO the mount (the operator's own VERIFY-1 contingency), never git-on-mount.' This closes the one real over-claim with the operator's own fallback. (2) Add a VERIFY gate mirroring REV3's MM2 skepticism but pointed at GDrive: 'Confirm Google Drive native file-version history actually retains usable point-in-time revisions for mounted .md edited via FUSE (not just for Docs/web edits), and survives rapid per-run commits; until confirmed, the off-host `git bundle` to a SECOND Drive folder / cold storage is the PRIMARY second recovery layer, and GDrive-revisions are a BONUS, not a relied-upon layer.' This makes "two independent recovery layers" true-by-construction rather than assumed. (3) Add the documented GDrive-sync corruption guard explicitly: exclude .git/ AND desktop.ini/.DS_Store/junk from the synced tree (the "desktop.ini injection" corruption class), and run `git fsck` in the nightly bundle job as a corruption tripwire (loud-failure, consistent with the AM durability guardrails). With these three edits the ruling's recommendation is unchanged and now fully evidence-defensible; net judgment stands — (C) hybrid with files+git canonical, single-live-writer + mkdir-lock collision-PREVENTION, one-way derived GDrive-revisions/optional-MM2-read-cache, additive-merge as manual /brain:recover-merge only, and MM2-canonical re-opened solely behind the operator's existing flip-trigger — remains strictly stronger than both REV3 single-store-on-MM2 and full-Jack bidirectional for a solo Meta AM.

votes.ruling:
Upheld. The files canonical hybrid is the strongest storage design for a solo Meta AM and survives attack. REV3 contradicts its own file canonical ship now recommendation, Jack bidirectional merge has a documented loss cost and breaks the single writer rule, and the hybrid keeps Jack lock while dropping the all pairs queue and demoting additive merge to recovery only. Files canonical dominates MM2 on durability. The lone flaw is wording, not design.</ruling>
<parameter name="reason">Could not refute it with Jack evidence. The one surviving attack is non fatal and wording only, that Jack genuine two writer setup justifies his sync for him but does not transfer to a single operator with one live writer. Minor nits: flag GDrive version history as unproven and label phases one and two as attended loops.</parameter>
<parameter name="refuted">false
votes.reason:
Could not refute it. Jack two writer setup justifies his sync for him but does not transfer to a single operator. Keep the hybrid.</parameter>
<parameter name="ruling">Upheld. Keep the files canonical hybrid; it is more durable than single store and safer than full bidirectional.</parameter>
<parameter name="better_alternative">None better. Keep the files canonical hybrid. Flag GDrive version history as unproven and label phases one and two as attended loops.</parameter>
</invoke>

votes.ruling: Refuted in part. The hybrid storage model holds, but the ruling overstates its win over REV3 on the unattended-capability lens.
votes.reason: The model is sound and the durability case against MM2-canonical is cited and real. But two of the ruling three comparative claims fail. Files-canonical now is not a correction of REV3, it is REV3 own approved ship-now option-3, so ships-sooner only means it equals REV3 today. And keep-the-capability-ceiling is false: the Phase-3 unattended path re-proposes a persistent devserver REV3 spike proved unprovisionable, and the Automations-as-nudge fallback only pings the operator to open the laptop, so the file-canonical ceiling is operator-attended. The ruling thus surrenders the one thing MM2-canonical uniquely buys, a laptop-closed daily cockpit per invariant I-10, while claiming to keep every strength. The trade is defensible since the operator ranks no-silent-loss first and MM2 unattended path is itself unreliable per its 23-run silent-stop bug, so the destination survives but the framing must concede the unattended lens and fix Phase 3.
votes.better_alternative: Tier-split hybrid, strictly stronger than both. Keep every other ruling element. Split the canonical decision by the two tiers the design already has. AUTHORED tier (profile, relationship, style, narrative, commitments, _system) stays files plus local git on the FUSE mount, with GDrive native revisions as a free second recovery layer and a nightly git bundle off-host for DR. This is the only un-regenerable truth, so it gets the proven recovery mechanisms and a single locked interactive writer. MIRROR and generated tier (performance, rs-ledger, scorecard, coverage, today, drafts) may be written by the unattended cron to an MM2-reachable target because it is fully regenerable, so MM2 truncation or a silent-stop costs nothing recoverable and the 0-files-written heartbeat guard catches the stop loudly. This unlocks the laptop-closed daily cockpit of invariant I-10 without betting durable truth on late-beta infra. The only cron write touching the AUTHORED tier, the 96h narrative append, is parked in the pending-suggestions handoff lane and drained interactively, so files plus git remain the sole AUTHORED writer. This dissolves REV3 pick-two trilemma because the trilemma only binds the regenerable tier, which does not need file-git durability. Corrected flip-trigger: promote the AUTHORED tier onto MM2 only when MM2 per-file cap is verified, retention is documented, the silent-stop bug is closed, and a redundancy story exists.

votes.ruling: HOLDS on the core decision; PARTIAL-REFUTE on two implementation-mechanism claims. The safety-governance DECISION — single-writer-now + gated-multi-agent-future, import Jack's coordination machinery (locks/task-queue/ownership-partition/danger-zone) as latent single-writer-safe infrastructure, and admit a true second writer only behind a numeric (8 green weeks) + structural (DSS matrix + disjoint writable partition) gate — is the genuinely strongest posture and is well-evidenced. Keep it. But two Layer-2/Layer-1 MECHANISM claims are unsound against the REV3-canonical substrate and must be corrected before this is implementation-ready.
votes.reason: Core decision survives every attack with strong evidence. (1) "Jack's fleet papers over races with additive-merge" is accurate, not a strawman: Jack's own manual concedes it — §16 "On true factual contradiction -> GDrive wins... Metamate's changes lost"; §10 "Metamate's T=0:00 changes are LOST unless LLM merge recovers them"; merge IS the recovery mechanism (§10 Phase 3 LLM additive merge), exactly the loss/fabrication vector AM Brain I-7 rejects. (2) Single-writer is 3-0 adversarially verified (Cognition P12) AND independently corroborated by Anthropic's production multi-agent research system, which I fetched: subagents are read-only, only the lead agent writes, and it runs them synchronously precisely because concurrent writes add coordination complexity ("this simplifies coordination") — the same ORCH-1 posture. (3) Fleet is premature for a solo AM: bottleneck is human review under rung-0 draft-only, not write throughput; a write-capable fleet buys nothing until autonomy graduates while adding the race+merge failure class. (4) Danger-zone hardening is a strict SUPERSET of Jack §17 — all seven catastrophic rows map to a ruling mechanism (split-brain eliminated entirely by single store; unlocked-write -> Layer-2 lock; wholesale-overwrite -> class+human_only_fields guard; delete -> operator-only+git/backup; corrupt-registry -> catastrophic list). (5) The human_only_fields import is a REAL evidence-backed improvement, not decoration: a pure file-class model (canonical's "file boundary = ownership boundary") genuinely cannot express "generated file EXCEPT these human-set fields," yet Jack's production system needed exactly that (§12 status/tier HUMAN ONLY; impact_override/ease_override NEVER touched) — so enumerating human_only_fields closes a hole DM-8 leaves latent. WHY STILL REFUTED (genuine, evidence-backed defects, both bound to the WRONG storage substrate): (A) Layer-2 item 4 proposes an MM2 "lock-sentinel via the memory API" as a mechanical mutex that makes the single-writer invariant "MECHANICALLY enforced." But REV3 (the canonical APPROVED decision) makes Metamate project memory canonical, and its own storage design guardrail states MM2 is eventually consistent ~minutes with "same-file = silent last-write-wins"; an API-written sentinel over an eventually-consistent store is NOT a reliable mutex (a second writer may not observe it for minutes). The ruling even cites "REV3 flags MM2 eventual-consistency" in the same item, then layers an API lock on that same store — internally inconsistent. REV3's actual enforcement is single-writer-BY-CONSTRUCTION (cron is the sole writer; human edits arrive via a different Collab-Files surface the cron never read-modify-writes) + per-file owner: frontmatter, not a lock. (B) The ownership TABLE is expressed as filesystem path-globs (clients/*/performance.md) consulted by a "write-guard on EVERY mutation," but those globs are the GDrive-markdown layout that REV3 DEMOTED to read-only backup; on the MM2 canonical store the enforcement surface is owner: frontmatter per the storage design doc, not a path-glob guard. The ownership CLASSES transfer cleanly; the binding MECHANISM is described against the superseded substrate. These defects do not touch the governance/autonomy/multi-agent posture (the stated attack focus) — Layers 3/4 are verbatim-faithful to canonical OUT-5/6 + SPEC GATE-1..5 with no drift — so on the attack focus the ruling holds; the refutation is scoped to two concurrency-mechanism claims that are unsound on the REV3 substrate.
votes.better_alternative: Keep the DECISION verbatim (single-writer-now, gated fleet, latent coordination infra, numeric+structural graduation gate) and fix only the two mechanism claims so they bind to the REV3-canonical substrate: (1) Restate Layer-2 concurrency as a SINGLE-WRITER-BY-CONSTRUCTION invariant on MM2 project memory enforced by per-file owner: frontmatter + the rule "cron never read-modify-writes a human-owned file" (REV3's own guardrail) — NOT an API-sentinel mutex; explicitly confine mkdir .lockdir to the GDrive nightly-backup mount and any future FS-agent path, and state plainly that it is a crash/reentrancy guard on the backup substrate, NOT a cross-store mutex over eventually-consistent MM2. Keep the lock primitive present (it is the ready-made enforcement for the gated future) but down-scope its claimed guarantee from "mechanically enforced mutual exclusion now" to "the latent primitive the disjoint-partition future will need." (2) Express the ownership table as substrate-parametric: classes + write_mode + human_only_fields are the contract, enforced as owner:/human_only_fields frontmatter on the MM2 canonical store and re-expressible as path-globs only on the markdown backup; the write-guard reads frontmatter, not FS path-globs, when MM2 is canonical. Also add one explicit line to the graduation gate: shared append-only logs (audit.log/event-log) under multiple writers need a single designated appender or atomic-append, since disjoint-partition logic covers overwrite/RMW conflicts but not concurrent appends to one log. With those three edits the ruling is implementation-ready and its core posture — which is correct — is unchanged.

votes.ruling: UPHELD with one required correction. The decision (single-operator-now, gated-multi-agent-future) is the strongest posture for a solo Meta AM at launch and at maturity, and is more strongly supported than the ruling argues. The decisive support the ruling under-uses: the operator is the sole human reviewer, and drafts-only rung-0 means review bandwidth, not write throughput, is the binding constraint, so a second concurrent writer produces drafts faster than one human can review, adding conflict risk for zero value. Jack adopts multiple agents for access-surface diversity (filesystem vs API vs mobile), not parallel writing. Danger-zone hardening is real: 6 of Jack's 7 catastrophics are closed or eliminated under the single store plus owner-partition plus operator-only delete plus git recovery. Required correction: the MM2-side lock-sentinel is unsound as a mutex and is oversold, and the only atomic lock now sits on the non-canonical backup, so the real single-writer guarantee must be relocated to a host-level single-writer-process singleton, with the in-store lock down-stated to advisory.
votes.reason: The core decision survives all three attacks. Fleet-premature: with one human reviewer under drafts-only autonomy, parallel writers out-produce review capacity, delivering no value and only conflict risk; Jack's stated fleet rationale is access-surface diversity not parallel writing; the ruling preserves all read value (read-only researchers plus read-only mobile lookup). Merge-claim: the ruling slightly overstates works-ONLY-via-merge (Jack also layers locks, ownership-matrix, task-queue, with merge as last resort), but the structural conclusion holds because Jack's per-store locks do NOT span the GDrive-to-MM2 boundary (manual section 15: Metamate should avoid editing files Claude Code recently modified), so the additive merge (section 16) is load-bearing precisely because of the two-store split, and that split is what makes a five-writer fleet possible at all; remove the second store and you remove the merge and the concurrent-write fleet by construction, so adopting it now would re-import the silent-loss and fabrication vector I-7/REV3 correctly rejects. Danger-zone: 6 of 7 catastrophics hardened, and the disjoint-writable-partition graduation gate is the genuinely strong move because two writers share no writable file, leaving no conflict to merge, which is exactly what lets the fleet return later without the merge. Genuine evidence-backed weaknesses found, but they hit a redundant supporting mechanism rather than the decision: W1 the MM2 lock-sentinel is unsound because by the ruling's own cited substrate MM2 is eventually consistent on the order of minutes (canonical line 245), so an in-store advisory flag gives no mutual exclusion (a second writer reads no-lock for minutes), which defeats the ruling's claims that no two overlapping runs interleave and that a future writer fails loud, and worse, under REV3 the only atomic lock (mkdir lockdir) lives on the GDrive backup which is no longer canonical while the canonical MM2 store gets only the weak sentinel, a surface mismatch the ruling never reconciles. W2 Layer 2 silently reverses AM Brain canonical's explicit no-locks no-tombstones git-replaces-WAL stance (lines 79, 130, 142, 226) and presents locks as parent-endorsed when it is actually a conflict-resolution against the parent. W3 the disjoint-partition kills write-conflicts but not cross-writer read-consistency at maturity (a generated file reads append-only and authored files a co-writer may mutate mid-run); the lock was meant to cover this but inherits W1, and it is deferred-by-construction so minor. W4 the rung-2 send gate (zero clearance failures in a 20-msg-per-week QA sample) may be unsatisfiable at solo send volume early. None of these refute the decision, because the single-writer invariant is guaranteed primarily by there being literally one writer process plus the owner-partition plus track-last-dispatch no-double-fire, not by the in-store lock; W1 is a required correction, not a refutation.
votes.better_alternative: Keep the decision verbatim; fix the lock layer and flag the parent-override. (A) Relocate the real mutex out of the eventually-consistent store: enforce single-writer with a host-level singleton so exactly one engine runs at a time (cron Automation OR interactive session, never both) via a host lockfile or PID-guard or a scheduler-held run token; this actually closes the cron-vs-interactive reentrancy gap the ruling rightly identifies. Down-state the MM2 sentinel to best-effort advisory plus stale-crash and reentrancy hint, NOT a mutex; keep the atomic mkdir lockdir only where it is sound, namely the FUSE/GDrive backup mount and any future filesystem agent; if MM2 exposes a conditional or compare-and-swap or ETag write, use that for true exclusion, and since unknown does not equal zero, do not assume it exists, gate on verifying it. (B) Make the owner-frontmatter partition plus cron-never-read-modify-writes-a-human-file (REV3's stated guardrail) the primary same-store safety, with the lock as defense-in-depth, not the reverse. (C) State Layer 2 explicitly as a conflict-resolution: synthesis OVERRIDES AM Brain canonical's no-locks stance; git and backup remain the recovery substrate, but a host-singleton plus advisory lock are added to close the cron-times-interactive reentrancy hole git does not cover, and this is the latent primitive the gated future reuses. (D) For the maturity ceiling, add a cross-writer read-consistency rule to the graduation prerequisites (snapshot or version-pin the append-only and authored inputs a generated-file writer reads, or serialize regeneration behind the host-singleton) so partitioned writers never produce from a torn read, folding it into the already-deferred Agent IO Security and coordination design. (E) Minor: scope the rung-2 and rung-3 send gate to N consecutive clean sends in a category rather than a fixed 20-msg-per-week QA sample so the gate is satisfiable at solo volume.

votes.ruling:
UPHELD (with 4 required precision/doc corrections; none overturn the decision). The safety-governance posture — single-writer-NOW, import Jack's coordination machinery (locks/task-queue/ownership-partition/danger-zone) as LATENT single-writer-safe infrastructure, admit a true second writer ONLY behind an explicit numeric+structural gate (8 green weeks + DSS×Destination matrix + disjoint writable partition), one canonical store with one-way backup + git, gate unbypassable, two explicit autonomy ladders — is the genuinely strongest posture for a solo Meta AM at both launch AND maturity, on the evidence.

WHY IT HOLDS (the ATTACK FOCUS, answered):
- Fleet-premature-at-launch is EVIDENCE-BACKED, not preference: Cognition P12 [HIGH, 3-0] ("multi-agent works when writes stay single-threaded; extra agents contribute intelligence not actions"); the ONLY production fleet datapoint (Jack) is a CAUTIONARY one — his 5-agent fleet IS his data-loss exposure (§16-17 "Metamate's changes are LOST unless LLM merge recovers them"), and the canonical's [JSB] provenance line explicitly drops "its write-side: bidirectional LLM-merge, 5-agent fleet, dual locks." The 164-silent-run disaster was "personal+bidirectional+silent" (a fleet+merge failure). So the ruling correctly refuses to rubber-stamp Jack's production-proven sync, AND correctly refuses to rubber-stamp REV3's bare single-store — it lands on the operator-flagged HYBRID (one canonical store + Jack's locking + task-queue + one-way backup + git version-history), which is exactly CRITICAL-CONTEXT #1's "on the table" option, with the one-way (not bidirectional) direction justified by the merge-loss record rather than asserted.
- Read-side value is NOT left on the table: Layer 5 keeps ALL of Jack's read-parallelism (evidence-finder, benchmark-fetcher, meeting-prep researcher, read-only mobile MyClaw/MetaClaw). Only WRITE-parallelism is deferred, and the throughput case for a second WRITER over 33 serialized-per-client (ORCH-3) accounts with a ~90%-cheap-exit Tier-2 filter is UNPROVEN (unknown≠zero, but no source establishes need). Deferral behind a gate that REUSES the parents' own Phase-5 criteria (SPEC line 505 + v2 §14 both = "8 consecutive green weeks") is proportionate, not over-conservative.
- It HARDENS the danger-zone, not just preserves it: it converts Jack's PROSE §17 catastrophic list into a MACHINE-CHECKABLE forbidden-ops list read as data by the write-guard, while ELIMINATING STRUCTURALLY (not by discipline) Jack's two worst modes — split-brain ("write to MM2 directly" can't exist: one store) and bidirectional-merge-loss (no merge). gate.py promoted into Jack's danger-zone framing = unbypassable send path. Every Jack catastrophic row maps to a hardened, logged, blocked control.
- human_only_fields is the ruling's strongest ORIGINAL contribution and is correct: DM-8 assigns ownership at FILE granularity ("performance.md → overwrite freely"), but Jack §7 shows human-set fields (tier/status/scorecard_status, impact_override/ease_override) living INSIDE engine-regenerated files, and Jack §12 carves them out as prose ("status and tier are HUMAN ONLY"). The ruling enumerates them as checkable human_only_fields — closing a hole AM-Brain leaves latent and mechanizing what Jack only states. Correctly tagged [Jack] carve-out / [synth] mechanization.
- Disjoint-writable-partition (item 14c) is the correct, formally-sound fix that is genuinely stronger than Jack: Jack partitions writes by SECTION (§12 one-owner-per-section) but his two writers are partitioned by STORE not by path, so both can write the same file from different stores → forced merge. Partition-by-writable-PATH across writers (enabled by the canonical's fine-grained dossier decomposition that already split Jack's monolith) means no shared writable file ⇒ no conflict ⇒ no re-imported merge.

REQUIRED CORRECTIONS (refinements, not defeaters — the synthesis doc must carry these):
(1) Phrasing: "Jack's fleet works ONLY by papering over write races with the bidirectional merge" slightly understates it — Jack has TWO race mechanisms on TWO axes: locks guard the SAME-store concurrent-FUSE race (Claude Code + Codex), the merge reconciles the CROSS-store race (GDrive↔MM2). Both are real fleet costs; the merge is the one I-7/REV3 deletes by collapsing to one store. The decision (drop merge, keep locks) is exactly right; only the one-liner needs tightening.
(2) The MM2 lock-sentinel's stale policy is weaker-than-stated: a lock built ON an eventually-consistent (~minutes) store has a ~minutes acquire/read race where two writers can both see "unlocked," so item 4's "no two overlapping runs interleave" is PROBABILISTIC, not absolute. Scope the lock in the doc as a crash/reentrancy + fail-loud guard (claims 4b/4c hold regardless of lag); retain REV3's "cron never read-modify-writes a human file" + owner: frontmatter as the belt to the lock's suspenders.
(3) Autonomous-archival's recovery substrate (git history + nightly one-way GDrive backup) depends on a REV3-OPEN production path under Metamate-native hosting (canonical line 246 flags engine-in-mission delivery + MM2 version-recovery/retention as unresolved). Carry that as a BLOCKING-OPEN for the archival-autonomy claim; the ruling's own downgrade-to-candidate-unless-git+backup-confirmed (item 3) is the correct defensive handling of that dependency, so keep it.
(4) Make explicit in _system/ownership.md a graduation-gate check that NO single generated file has two agent-writers under the then-current skill→agent mapping (e.g. Jack's context.md had 4 section-writers; the canonical's decomposition makes file-disjoint achievable, but the gate must verify it for the actual fleet config).
votes.reason: I tried hard to break it across the ATTACK FOCUS (fleet premature vs value left on table; does it harden the danger-zone) and the storage/sync axis the operator flagged as contested. The DECISION survives because every load-bearing element is evidence-grounded, not preference: Cognition P12 (3-0) for single-writer; Jack's OWN §16-17 merge-loss + the 164-silent-run record as the cautionary fleet datapoint that justifies NOT adopting his concurrent-write sync; the SPEC's existing 8-green-week Phase-5 gate reused as the second-writer gate; Jack §12/§15/§17 imported faithfully as coordination machinery. It is genuinely stronger than EITHER parent: it adds Jack's machine-checkable coordination + the human_only_fields carve-out that pure AM-Brain leaves latent at file granularity (verified: Jack §7 frontmatter mixes human-only tier/status into engine-regenerated files; §12 carves them as prose), while ELIMINATING STRUCTURALLY Jack's two catastrophic modes (split-brain, bidirectional-merge loss) rather than disciplining them away. Crucially it does NOT rubber-stamp REV3's bare single-store — it augments it with exactly the hybrid (one store + locks + queue + one-way backup + git) that CRITICAL-CONTEXT #1 put on the table, justifying one-way over bidirectional by evidence. Read-side fleet value is kept now; only unproven-needed write-parallelism is deferred behind a real gate. The four weaknesses I found are all PRECISION/DOCUMENTATION defects (loose 'works ONLY by' phrasing; MM2 lock staleness is probabilistic not absolute on an eventually-consistent store; archival recovery substrate depends on a REV3-OPEN engine-in-mission/MM2-retention path; the disjoint-partition gate should add an explicit 'no two agent-writers per generated file' check) — each argues for a footnote or a stated blocking-open, none overturns single-writer-now / gated-fleet-future / one-canonical-store. Under the rule 'refuted=true ONLY for a genuine evidence-backed weakness in the decision,' the decision holds; the defects are corrective, so refuted=false with the four corrections attached as required.
votes.better_alternative: No stronger DECISION exists; the ruling already lands on the operator-flagged hybrid. The only improvements are the 4 required corrections above, plus one optional strengthening: state the L2 concurrency model as DEFENSE-IN-DEPTH with an explicit precedence — (a) owner: frontmatter = static single-writer-per-file policy [REV3, primary]; (b) disjoint writable partition across writers = no shared writable path [synth, the conflict-eliminator]; (c) lock-sentinel = dynamic crash/reentrancy + fail-loud guard for the cron-vs-interactive-same-owner overlap [Jack §15, the only realistic launch contention]; (d) REV3 'cron never read-modify-writes a human file' = scheduling backstop where the lock's MM2 propagation race could leak. Layering (a)-(d) and naming which defends what (rather than presenting the lock as a standalone mutex) closes the one place the ruling oversells a mechanism, without changing the decision. Also fold the 'one-way not bidirectional' justification into a single explicit conflict-resolution line citing the 164-run + Jack merge-loss evidence, so the storage decision reads as evidence-driven rather than as an inherited REV3 assertion.

votes.ruling:
PARTIALLY UPHELD with two required corrections. The CORE ruling — a decomposed per-aspect dossier (AM Brain) is the strongest layout for an agent-written compounding store, NOT Jack's single rich per-account context.md — holds firmly and is independently corroborated. But two file-grain choices INSIDE the ruling (rs-ledger.md as one file; narrative.md uncapped) re-create the very single-rich-file hazard the ruling exists to eliminate, so the ruling as written is not yet the strongest form of its own thesis.

WHAT SURVIVES (do not change): (1) Decompose the dossier into small single-aspect, single-owner files [AM]. External evidence I verified is directly on-point: agent-memory engineering recommends "structure memory as separate files per topic to reduce contention," and file-editing research warns "repeated/similar sections make the model treat parts of the file as redundant and silently drop them" — a direct hit AGAINST Jack's 13-section monolith and FOR decomposition. Claude Code's own production memory model is "one file per memory," i.e. the harness this runs on already validates the pattern. (2) The per-FILE write-class scheme (human-owned/AM-confirmed/generated/append-only with one owner: per file) is the actual merge-safety mechanism: aspects never share a file, so field-level last-write-wins is structurally impossible — strictly better than Jack's section-ownership inside ONE file, which still serializes every writer on one inode. (3) Worked fixtures prove decomposed files are tiny (0.7-2.6 KB each; ~10 KB/account total), so the "N reads" cost is negligible and nowhere near the 100 KB MM2 ceiling. (4) GENUINE synthesis improvement over BOTH parents: the ruling adds a generated, READ-ONLY per-account clients/<slug>/context.md rollup. This takes Jack's single-glance ergonomic (his strongest win) and inverts its semantics to a projection over the decomposed truth — neutralizing the only serious objection to decomposition (no single glance / many reads for cheap Tier-2). Operator-editability is BETTER, not worse: editing relationship.md cannot clobber performance.md in Collab Files, and read-only rollups stop the operator hand-editing a steamrolled file. This is not a rubber-stamp of REV3; it is a real best-of-both.

WHAT MUST BE FIXED (the two evidence-backed weaknesses): (A) rs-ledger.md = "ONE file, all RS as YAML list ... history" silently re-imports the monolith hazard. It is agent-rewritten daily, structurally repetitive (N near-identical per-RS YAML blocks = the exact "silently drop redundant blocks" trigger from the evidence), append-only-history-unbounded as won/lost accumulate, and reaches ~86 KB at a concentrated 100-RS account (vs the ruling's own 100 KB cap). Jack himself decomposes finer here (pipeline/rs-{name}.md, one file per RS); the ruling moved to a COARSER grain against its own principle. (B) narrative.md is "cumulative reverse-chron," DUAL-LOADED every query, subject to the 100 KB ceiling, yet has NO roll-up/cap — while every sibling (raw CIs, performance) is bounded. Jack sidestepped this by keeping the meeting log in an EXTERNAL Google Doc (outside the ceiling); pulling it in-vault without a roll-up guarantees a long-tenured account eventually breaches the ceiling and bloats every dual-load.
votes.reason: The headline decision (decomposed dossier beats Jack's single rich context.md) is correct and independently corroborated by external evidence — I could not refute it; it is the genuinely stronger design for an agent-written, compounding store on agent-write-reliability, merge-safety, retrieval, AND operator-editability. So I do NOT overturn the ruling. But I found two real, specific, evidence-backed weaknesses where the ruling contradicts its OWN logic, which is why refuted=true (scoped, not wholesale): (1) rs-ledger.md as ONE file with append-only history and no open/closed split is precisely the structurally-repetitive, agent-rewritten, ceiling-approaching monolith the decomposition principle exists to kill — the file-editing evidence on silent-drop of repeated blocks applies directly to N identical RS YAML blocks, and Jack himself uses finer one-file-per-RS grain here, so the ruling regressed against both its principle and the production parent. (2) narrative.md is the one in-vault file with no roll-up/cap despite being dual-loaded every query and bound by the same 100 KB ceiling the ruling enforces elsewhere; Jack avoided this by externalizing it. These are concrete and fixable. Both fixes (per-RS files or open-vs-archive split + cap for the ledger; annual narrative roll-up mirroring the existing CI-digest pattern) preserve and complete the ruling rather than reversing it. The single-store/markdown-git-backup memory-model frame and the merged registry/freshness/write-policy machinery are sound and unaffected.
votes.better_alternative: Keep the decomposed-dossier + generated-rollup design exactly as ruled, with two grain corrections that finish the thesis: (1) Replace single rs-ledger.md with a directory clients/<slug>/rs/ — one file per open RS (rs-<rs_id>.md, mirror tier, agent-overwrite-free) PLUS rs-archive/<year>.md for won/lost (append-only, rolled out of the live set on close). This restores Jack's [Jack] finer grain at the one place the ruling regressed, eliminates the repeated-block silent-drop trigger, caps every file far below 100 KB, and lets the daily loop overwrite ONE RS without rewriting the whole ledger. The generated clients/<slug>/context.md rollup already gives the operator the single-glance RS summary, so nothing is lost ergonomically. (2) Add a narrative roll-up to the ALG-7 hygiene sweep: when narrative.md crosses a size/age threshold, fold entries older than ~12 months into clients/<slug>/narrative-archive/<year>.md (read-only, still grep-able, still backed up), keeping the dual-loaded narrative.md to a recent rolling window — exactly mirroring the interactions raw->digest->archive pattern the design already uses. Net: the strongest layout becomes "every agent-written, growth-bearing artifact is decomposed OR roll-up-bounded; every multi-instance set (RS, CIs) is one-file-per-instance; one generated read-only context.md per account is the single-glance projection." That is strictly stronger than the ruling as written and still beats Jack's monolith on every attacked axis.

votes.ruling: HOLDS. The decomposed per-aspect dossier (per-FILE ownership) + auto-built context.md rollup-as-index is genuinely the strongest layout for an AGENT-WRITTEN, compounding store, and beats Jack's single rich context.md-per-account on all four attacked dimensions. Adopt the proposed file/memory layout as written, with two non-blocking caveats folded in (caveats, not refutations).
votes.reason: I tried hard to refute and could not find an evidence-backed weakness that overturns the layout. (1) RETRIEVAL/RELIABILITY: external best practice is explicit that a single large memory file "eats tokens every session and the model follows it less reliably," and that multiple well-organized files with a clear index beat one big file (readyht/MatrixOrigin/Oracle, 2026) [ext]. Jack's context.md is ~13 owned sections + frontmatter all growing in one file = exactly that failure shape; the ruling's per-aspect files + generated context.md rollup IS the recommended index pattern, recovering Jack's one-glance benefit as a generated artifact. (2) AGENT-WRITE-RELIABILITY + MERGE-SAFETY (the crux): external evidence's strongest caution is that atomic whole-file rewrite causes silent "lost-in-the-middle" content loss; the ruling neutralizes this by aligning write-mode to volatility — only the MIRROR/generated tier (performance, rs-ledger, context.md, scorecard) is full-overwritten, and those are deterministically regenerated by the Python engine from systems-of-record, so a dropped line is re-derived next run, not lost; the AUTHORED tier that actually compounds (narrative, relationship, profile, commitments) is append-only or diff-proposed, never blind-overwritten. Jack's OWN danger-zone (§17/§10) condemns his own design ("Overwrite context.md wholesale loses additions; always merge/append") and forces a bidirectional LLM additive merge that the canonical doc I-7 and external practice both flag as the fabricate/lose anti-pattern. The ruling deletes the merge entirely (in-vault single-truth narrative; external GDoc demoted to ingestion source; one single-threaded writer + per-file lock) — strictly safer. (3) OPERATOR-EDITABILITY: registry stays YAML w/ comments [Jack-preserving]; each human-owned concern is a small one-page file an operator can read/correct vs hunting a section in a 13-section file. The single real cost I found — cross-file consistency drift (e.g. relationship.current_objection vs rs-ledger client_readiness) — is genuine but (a) mitigated because the engine DERIVES the dependent field (ALG-5) under a single writer, so it is a computed dependency not two hand-kept copies, and (b) NOT solved by Jack's single file either, whose independently-owned sections can equally contradict with no consistency check. So decomposition is no worse there and better elsewhere. Refuting would be contrarian, not rigorous.
votes.better_alternative: No stronger LAYOUT exists; keep it. Two refinements to fold in as caveats: (A) SOURCING-PRECISION NIT — the header's bare "markdown+git = one-way backup per REV3" overstates settledness; the brief explicitly flags storage/sync as contested, and the canonical doc's own §11 'Recommended L6 path' actually ships option 3 (laptop, file+git CANONICAL, I-7 intact) NOW while REV3-approving Metamate-native. The layout ruling is correctly HOST-AGNOSTIC (identical file tree + ownership classes whether canonical = MM2 project-memory or GDrive file+git), so phrase it as 'one canonical store; the decomposed tree + write-policy classes are portable across either host decision' and stop asserting the host outcome inside the memory-model ruling. (B) ADD A CROSS-FILE CONSISTENCY GUARD that converts the one residual weakness into a strength: a deterministic engine reconciliation step (consistency lint) where every build asserts derived fields agree with their authored source (e.g. rs-ledger.client_readiness == f(relationship.md)), surfacing any mismatch in the ALG-7 hygiene sweep / cockpit rather than silently diverging — pin it with a producer->consumer safeParse-style test [synth]. Neither changes the layout; both harden it.

votes.ruling:
KEEP the decomposed per-aspect dossier (the core architecture is correct), but FIX three concrete, evidence-backed defects in the ruling as written.

CENTRAL CLAIM HOLDS — decomposition beats Jack's single context.md for an agent-WRITTEN compounding store, and Jack's own production manual is the strongest evidence FOR it. Across the four attack vectors:
- Write-reliability [RULING WINS]: Jack [§7/§12] packs ~13 differently-owned sections into ONE context.md written by many skills (/refresh overwrites Snapshot/Brand/Spend; /post-meeting rewrites Account Context; /pitch-priorities overwrites Recommended Solutions). The unreliable LLM operation is surgical in-file section-rewrite. Decomposition makes ownership a FILE property, so every write becomes whole-file regenerate of a single-class file — the reliable primitive under I-1 single-writer.
- Merge-safety [RULING WINS decisively]: Jack needs the .locks/ mkdir protocol [§15], LLM additive-merge on conflict [§16], and the explicit "Overwrite context.md wholesale = catastrophic" danger-zone entry [§17] ONLY BECAUSE he is monolithic. Class-per-file dissolves all of it: the writer never parses-and-splices a shared file.
- Retrieval [≈ even]: per-aspect files give minimal-context loads (ORCH-4); RET-1 scoping is by clients/<slug>/ either way. Decomposition does add read-amplification on MM2 (N file-fetches vs Jack's 1), but the generated rollup clients/<slug>/context.md bounds it to one fetch for most reads. Not a refutation.
- Operator-editability [RULING WINS on SAFE editability]: the human surface is a small, bounded authored tier (5 one-page files) cleanly separated from the regenerable surface, so the operator cannot accidentally edit a generated section and the cron cannot clobber a human edit — the exact interleaving that makes Jack's wholesale-overwrite dangerous. Jack wins only on one-pane convenience, which loses to safety for an agent-written store.

DEFECTS (must fix, do not ship as-is):
1) NESTING MISCOUNT (load-bearing). The ruling declares "enforce ... <3-level nesting" and asserts "clients/<slug>/interactions/raw/ is exactly 3 levels — the deepest allowed." It is 4 directory levels (clients/slug/interactions/raw), so the ruling's own canonical path VIOLATES the MM2 <3-level rule it claims to enforce. Under REV3 MM2 is canonical, so per-level traversal cost ("one call per level", Jack §17) binds. FIX: flatten to clients/<slug>/interactions/raw-<YYYY-MM-DD>-<type>.md (interactions becomes the only sub-level → 3 levels).
2) "git-versioned authored tier" MISLABEL. Under REV3 the live canonical store is Metamate project memory, NOT a filesystem; git versions only the nightly one-way GDrive backup. Live version-recovery is MM2's own and is an OPEN/UNVERIFIED item per the REV3 changelog. FIX: reword to "durable tier; backed up nightly to git (one-way); live recovery = MM2 retention (open)".
3) "per-file single-writer LOCK (owner: frontmatter)" OVERSTATES the guarantee. REV3 states MM2 has no real lock — frontmatter owner is an advisory single-writer convention with a silent last-write-wins backstop. FIX: call it "single-writer DISCIPLINE; last-write-wins backstop." (Decomposition is what makes this weak guarantee sufficient — so it strengthens, not weakens, the case.)
votes.reason: The decomposed-dossier decision is genuinely strongest and survives scrutiny — and is corroborated by Jack's production evidence, whose lock+merge+danger-zone apparatus exists only to compensate for his monolithic context.md. But the ruling AS WRITTEN contains a concrete, checkable, evidence-backed defect: its own canonical path clients/<slug>/interactions/raw/ is 4 directory levels, which violates the <3-level MM2 nesting rule the same paragraph declares it enforces; under REV3 (MM2 = canonical store) per-level traversal cost binds, so this is load-bearing, not cosmetic. Two adjacent REV2→REV3 narration-drift mislabels compound it: the authored tier is tagged 'git-versioned' (git only versions the nightly one-way backup; live recovery is MM2's own, unverified) and protected by a per-file 'lock' (MM2 owner-frontmatter is advisory single-writer with silent last-write-wins, not an enforced lock). Per the instruction to set refuted=true on a genuine evidence-backed weakness, these defects qualify — while the architecture itself is correct and should be kept with the fixes applied.
votes.better_alternative: The SAME decomposed-dossier ruling with three edits: (1) flatten the deepest path to clients/<slug>/interactions/raw-<YYYY-MM-DD>-<type>.md so the tree is provably ≤3 levels and honors the MM2 traversal limit it cites; (2) replace 'git-versioned authored tier' with 'durable tier, backed up nightly one-way to git; live version-recovery = MM2 retention (open item per REV3)'; (3) replace 'per-file single-writer lock (owner: frontmatter)' with 'single-writer discipline per file; MM2 backstop = silent last-write-wins'. No different memory model is warranted — decomposition is the right call; precisely because REV3's MM2 store offers only a weak last-write-wins guarantee with no enforced lock, class-per-file (one writer-class per file, whole-file regenerate for generated/mirror, propose-diff for authored, append+tombstone for ledgers) is MORE necessary than under a filesystem, since it is what makes that weak guarantee sufficient without any merge or lock.
